metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The plan asserts the adapter reads Claude Code session transcript JSONL and extracts a `ts` field (ISO-8601) plus error-bearing text fields, but never specifies (a) the filesystem path where Claude Code stores its session transcripts or the discovery mechanism, and (b) the JSONL record schema (which field holds the error text, which field holds the timestamp). The repo documents the Codex transcript path (`~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*-<id>.jsonl`) but contains no analogous specification for Claude Code transcripts. The implementation cannot locate the files or parse records without this information — these are the core integration surface, not incidentals.
findings.location: Approach section — 'error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL'; Emitted record schema — `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>`; AC1 — 'fixture JSONL transcript'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Transcript format' subsection to Approach: name the path glob (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/*.jsonl`), specify the relevant JSONL fields (e.g., `content[].text` for message text, `timestamp` for ISO-8601 ts), and note whether the grep pass operates on raw lines or parsed field values. Reference the Claude Code documentation or a sample record. If the schema is not yet verified, mark this as requiring a one-session probe (cat one transcript file) before implementation — this is a low-cost verification, not a full SPIKE.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Files section to reflect that `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` already exists (from ticket 1f77) and must be EXTENDED (or left as-is), not created from scratch — only `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py` is genuinely new.
evidence: Directory listing of `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` returns `__init__.py` and `github_actions.py` — the `__init__.py` already exists.
Reading `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` shows it was created by ticket 1f77 with a module docstring establishing the isolation contract.

Plan says 'new isolated adapter (imported only by the backfill script, never by the core command)' — the existing `__init__.py` already carries this contract, so the plan incorrectly implies the file must be created from scratch.
findings.finding: The Files section labels `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` as a 'new' file, but it already exists (created by ticket 1f77) with content establishing the adapters isolation contract. The plan's assertion that this is a new file is empirically refuted by a direct directory read.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py, src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py — new isolated adapter`
scenarios: An implementer creating `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` from scratch would overwrite the existing file and its 1f77-established isolation docstring/contract.
If the implementer notices the file exists and skips creating it, they may be confused about whether the plan's intent (new content vs. extend) is met.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Files entry to: "`src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` — already exists (ticket 1f77); no change needed. `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py` — new isolated adapter module."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing, verify and document the Claude Code session transcript filesystem path and JSONL field schema (e.g., by inspecting a real transcript file); add a `## Transcript format` subsection to the plan naming the concrete path pattern and the field names (`ts` or equivalent, content field) that the grep and schema extraction rely on.
evidence: Plan states 'error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' but never names the transcript directory path.
Emitted schema includes `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>` — implies a known field name in source JSONL, unverified.
docs/session-id-shims.md names Codex transcript path (~/.codex/sessions/…) but has no Claude Code equivalent.
Repo experiment harnesses reference ~/.claude/jobs/<id>/tmp (scratch artifacts) — explicitly noted as NOT a transcript path in mine_outcome_corpus.py.
No file in the repo documents the Claude Code session transcript JSONL location or field schema.
Evidence class: UNVERIFIED — could not probe the external format from available tools.
findings.finding: The plan's core mechanic — grep error signatures from Claude Code session transcript JSONL files and extract a `ts` field — depends on two unverified external facts: (1) the filesystem path where Claude Code stores session transcripts (never named in the plan or anywhere in the codebase), and (2) the JSONL record schema (field names, content structure). The repo documents the Codex transcript path (`~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*-<id>.jsonl`) in `docs/session-id-shims.md`, but no equivalent Claude Code transcript location or schema is cited. The emitted record uses `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>`, implying a specific field name exists in the source — unverified. If either assumption is wrong the adapter silently produces zero records or crashes.
findings.location: Approach section ('the transcripts are a near-complete local record… error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL'); Emitted record JSON schema (`"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>`)
scenarios: Claude Code stores transcripts at a path the adapter does not know (e.g., ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/conversations.jsonl or similar), so the script finds no files and silently produces zero output with no error.
The JSONL `ts` field has a different name or format than assumed (e.g., `timestamp`, `created_at`, Unix epoch integer), causing the adapter to crash or emit malformed records.
A future Claude Code version changes the transcript format; since the format was never pinned to a spec, the adapter breaks silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Transcript format` subsection to the plan citing the concrete path (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/<project-id>/<session-id>.jsonl` or the actual observed path) and a representative JSONL record excerpt showing the timestamp field name and content structure. Pin the adapter to that schema explicitly, and add a unit-test fixture that exactly mirrors a real transcript record.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default transcript directory path (or document that `--transcript-dir` is a required CLI argument) in the plan so an executor has a concrete starting point.
evidence: 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — new: targeted extraction (grep error signatures) -> classify -> write labeled snapshots'
ABSENCE: no mention of a default path, CLI flag, or env var for locating the transcript JSONL files on the host machine
findings.finding: The backfill script's default real-world transcript directory path is not stated. The plan specifies a 'fixture transcript dir' for tests but never says where `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` should look by default on the actual machine (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/`, a CLI `--transcript-dir` argument with a documented default, or an env var). An executing agent must invent this convention.
findings.location: ## Files — `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`; ## Approach paragraph 1
scenarios: Executor implements the script with a hardcoded path that differs from where Claude Code actually stores transcripts, silently finding no files and producing no output.
Executor adds a `--transcript-dir` argument but picks a wrong default, requiring a follow-up correction before the script is usable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach or Files section: e.g., 'The script accepts `--transcript-dir` (default: `~/.claude/projects/`) as its sole required input; if the path does not exist, it exits with a warning and 0 records emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `none` is an internal-only routing state (never appears as `.kind` in any emitted record) or a suppressible output value; if internal-only, specify that the enum definition in code should still include it as a sentinel but output serialization filters it before write.
evidence: Routing table: '`none` ← a candidate line matching no rule above (dropped, not emitted)'
AC: 'test asserts the enum has exactly these values' listing `none` as one of the five
Output schema defines `kind` as 'one of the enum values' — does this include `none`?
ABSENCE: no statement clarifying none is an internal state vs. a suppressible output value
findings.finding: The `none` enum value is declared both as a member of the classification enum AND as a classification that is 'dropped, not emitted' — creating a structural contradiction about whether records with kind=none are ever materialized in output. The AC asserts the enum has exactly these five values (including `none`), but no emitted record ever carries kind=none. It is unspecified whether `none` is an internal routing state only or a suppressible emitted value.
findings.location: Approach section — Normative pattern→enum routing table; Emitted record JSON schema; AC bullet 4
scenarios: Implementor includes `none` as a valid `.kind` in emitted records, breaking the 'dropped, not emitted' contract.
Implementor excludes `none` from the enum definition, breaking the AC enum-membership assertion.
A jq query targeting `.kind` on real output encounters an unexpected `none` value.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: '`none` is an internal routing sentinel — it appears in the Python enum definition but is never written to output. The classifier silently discards candidate lines that reach this state.' This resolves both the enum-membership AC and the drop-not-emit rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `signature` field definition to specify exactly what it contains: either the pattern key/name from the routing table (e.g. `"ModuleNotFoundError"`) or the raw matched substring from the transcript line, with a concrete example.
evidence: Schema: '"signature": <the matched pattern/string>'
AC: 'jq -e assertions target .kind and .source/.confidence' — .signature is listed in the schema but AC does not specify what value to assert, hiding the ambiguity
ABSENCE: no example value for the signature field is given anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `signature` field in the emitted record schema is defined as `<the matched pattern/string>` — ambiguous between (a) the literal regex/pattern string from the routing table (e.g. `"ModuleNotFoundError"`) and (b) the actual matched substring extracted from the transcript line. This distinction is load-bearing for any `jq -e` assertion on `.signature` and for any consumer interpreting what the field represents.
findings.location: Approach section — Emitted record JSON schema
scenarios: Implementor writes the regex pattern name ('ModuleNotFoundError') into .signature; consumer expecting the extracted text gets an unusable value.
Implementor writes the full matched line excerpt; test fixture assertion on .signature fails because it expected the pattern name.
Two independent implementors produce incompatible outputs for the same transcript.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: '"signature": <the routing-table pattern key that matched, e.g. "ModuleNotFoundError">'. If the raw substring is preferred, say: '"signature": <the verbatim matched substring from the transcript line, e.g. "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named foo">'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) that explicitly exercises the `none` path: provide a fixture transcript line that matches the grep pre-filter (e.g. contains 'error') but satisfies none of the routing rules, and assert that zero records are emitted for it.
evidence: Approach: 'none ← a candidate line matching no rule above (dropped, not emitted)'
AC1: 'a clean transcript yields 0' — covers the no-grep-hit case only, not the grep-hit-but-no-rule-match case
No AC asserts behavior when a line matches the grep pre-filter but falls through all routing rules
findings.finding: The `none` classification path — where a transcript line passes the error-signature grep but matches no routing rule — is described in the Approach section ('dropped, not emitted') but has no covering acceptance criterion. AC1 only tests 'clean transcript yields 0' (no grep hits at all), leaving this distinct code path untested by the AC set.
findings.location: Approach (routing table, `none` entry) vs. AC line 1 ('a clean transcript yields 0')
scenarios: A transcript line contains e.g. a bare 'error' keyword that passes the grep pre-filter but matches none of the five routing rules — the adapter silently drops it; no AC verifies this path executes correctly and emits nothing.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 with a third fixture case: 'a transcript line containing a generic grep-matched keyword but no routing-rule match yields 0 emitted records (the line is dropped, not emitted).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test idempotency behavior for `backfill_transcripts.py` on re-run: either deduplicate by `(ts, signature)` key before writing, or use content-addressed output filenames, and add an AC assertion that running the script twice over the same fixture dir yields the same record set as running it once.
evidence: Plan says the script will 'write labeled snapshots' but never specifies whether output is idempotent (e.g., content-addressed filenames, deduplication by ts+signature) or whether re-running appends duplicates.
AC 3 only asserts that records ARE emitted, not that re-running yields the same set (no idempotency assertion).
T9 checklist: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically; operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once.'
findings.finding: The plan defines no idempotency behavior for `backfill_transcripts.py` on re-run: if the script is executed more than once against the same transcript directory, the emitted snapshot records will either accumulate as duplicates (append mode) or silently overwrite prior output (overwrite mode). Neither behavior is specified, leaving the update/ownership lifecycle incomplete and the output potentially corrupted or inflated under retry.
findings.location: Approach section — 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — new: targeted extraction ... -> write labeled snapshots'; AC bullet 3 — 'a test invokes its entry point over a fixture transcript dir and asserts it emits the labeled classified records'
scenarios: A developer runs `backfill_transcripts.py` once to populate snapshots, then runs it again after a new transcript session — duplicate records for the original sessions appear in the output, inflating env-toil counts without any error or warning.
findings.suggested_fix: Deduplicate emitted records by `(ts, signature)` before writing, and add a test that invokes the entry point twice over the same fixture dir and asserts the output is identical to a single run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output location and retirement policy for snapshot files (e.g., write to a single versioned file that is overwritten, or document a `--clean` flag, or at minimum document that the output directory must be manually managed).
evidence: Plan names output as 'labeled snapshots' but never specifies output directory, file naming convention, or any cleanup/expiry policy.
T9 checklist: 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate.'
The plan explicitly frames this as a one-time or occasional backfill, yet provides no mechanism to prevent accumulation across runs.
findings.finding: The plan defines no RETIRE/cleanup path for the snapshot files written by `backfill_transcripts.py`. Over time (or across multiple backfill runs), these files will accumulate indefinitely on the local filesystem with no expiry, rotation, or removal mechanism specified.
findings.location: Approach section — 'write labeled snapshots'; Files section — no mention of output location or cleanup
scenarios: A developer running the script periodically accumulates unbounded snapshot files in whatever output directory the script targets, with no way to identify stale/superseded records short of manual inspection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC fixtures and assertions for at least the `integration` and `dependency` classification branches (e.g., a transcript line containing `ConnectionError` classifies as `integration`; one containing `Could not find a version` classifies as `dependency`), so the routing table's correctness is enforced end-to-end across all active branches.
evidence: AC1 specifies only 'a transcript with a known env-failure signature (e.g. a ModuleNotFoundError line) is extracted and deterministically classified into at least one interval tagged env_setup'
Routing table defines four active enum values (env_setup, dependency, integration, tooling) each with multiple patterns
No AC mentions fixtures or assertions for dependency (pip/uv/version conflict), integration (ConnectionError/429/LLMUnavailableError), or tooling (git/ruff/mypy/Permission denied) branches
findings.finding: The test plan exercises only one of the four active classification branches (env_setup via ModuleNotFoundError). The normative routing table defines three additional active branches — dependency, integration, and tooling — each with distinct regex/keyword patterns and real-world failure signatures, yet no AC specifies a fixture or assertion for any of them. A bug in the dependency or integration classifier (e.g., a mis-anchored regex or wrong enum assignment) would not be caught.
findings.location: Tests section / Acceptance Criteria AC1 / Approach 'Normative pattern→enum routing table'
scenarios: A regex typo in the integration branch (e.g., mismatched LLMUnavailableError pattern) leaves all integration failures unclassified in production backfill with no failing test
A copy-paste error swapping dependency and tooling enum assignments goes undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 (or add AC1b) with two additional fixture cases: (1) a JSONL line containing `ConnectionError` → assert classified record has `kind=integration, source=backfill_classified`; (2) a line containing `Could not find a version` → assert `kind=dependency`. Optionally add a `tooling` case (e.g., `Permission denied`). The `none` drop path is already implicitly covered by the clean-transcript → 0 assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite or embed a concrete reference to the Claude Code transcript file format (path pattern, JSONL schema, field names including the timestamp field) — ideally a snippet of a real transcript or a link to Claude Code documentation — so the grep-and-classify approach is grounded in verified capability rather than assumed.
evidence: Plan states 'an error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' as a given fact with no citation to Claude Code documentation about transcript file location or schema.
The emitted record schema assumes `.ts` comes from 'ISO-8601 of the transcript entry' — implying a known field name, but no spec is linked.
No mention of which Claude Code version(s) this is validated against, or whether the format is stable across versions.
Absence: no reference to Claude Code SDK docs, a sample transcript, or any prior validation of the assumed JSONL structure.
findings.finding: The plan asserts Claude Code session transcripts are stored as JSONL files with a stable, greppable schema (timestamps, content lines) but cites no documentation or specification for this format. If the actual transcript format differs (e.g., nested JSON objects, binary encoding, field names differ from assumed, or path changed across Claude Code versions), the grep-and-classify pipeline fails silently or crashes with no defined fallback.
findings.location: Approach section — 'transcript JSONL (patterns such as …)' and AC line 1 — 'fixture JSONL transcript'
scenarios: Claude Code stores transcripts in a different format (e.g., SQLite, binary protobuf, or deeply nested JSON) — the grep produces zero matches silently.
The timestamp field name or ISO-8601 assumption is wrong — the adapter emits records with null/missing `ts` fields, failing the output schema.
A future Claude Code update changes the transcript path or format — the backfill script breaks with no clear error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Transcript format' subsection to the Approach section that (1) states the known file path pattern (e.g. `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/session.jsonl`), (2) shows a representative JSONL line structure with actual field names, and (3) notes the Claude Code version(s) this was validated against. The fixture JSONL in tests should be sampled from or structurally identical to a real transcript entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` resumable/idempotent by either (a) writing a checkpoint file recording the last-processed transcript offset and resuming from it on re-run, or (b) including a stable deduplication key (e.g. `{ts, signature, source}` hash) in each emitted record and skipping records whose key already exists in the NDJSON store.
evidence: Plan states the backfill 'write[s] labeled snapshots' but specifies no mechanism to detect or skip records already written in a prior run.
snapshot.py `write_snapshot` always appends: `with path.open('a', ...) as fh: fh.write(line + '\n')` — no deduplication.
ABSENCE: no mention of idempotency key, checkpoint/progress file, or pre-write existence check.
findings.finding: The backfill script is not idempotent: re-running it after a partial failure will double-write all records that were already appended to the NDJSON store before interruption, because `write_snapshot` is append-only and the plan specifies no deduplication key, checkpoint file, or 'already-written' guard.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py; Approach section; snapshot.py `write_snapshot`
scenarios: Backfill runs, writes 200 records, then crashes. Re-running from the start appends another 200 records (or more), inflating the NDJSON store with duplicates that silently skew every downstream metric query.
findings.suggested_fix: Before appending, compute a deterministic `run_id` (hash of `ts + signature + source`) for each candidate record, scan existing NDJSON lines for that key, and skip if already present. Alternatively, write a `.rebar/backfill-transcripts.checkpoint` file recording the byte offset of the last successfully processed transcript line.

findings.finding: The plan specifies no idempotency or resumability mechanism for scripts/backfill_transcripts.py. If the script is interrupted mid-run or re-run after new transcripts accumulate, it will re-process already-classified transcript entries and emit duplicate classified records into the output, with no deduplication guard specified.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py (Files section) and Approach section
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the pattern in scripts/backfill_bug_class.py: write output to a fixed NDJSON artifact (e.g., .rebar/backfill-transcripts.ndjson), read already-emitted (ts, signature) pairs on startup, and skip any candidate that matches an already-seen pair. Assert in AC3 that a second run over the same fixture dir produces 0 new records.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note (or lightweight mechanism) for how the routing-table patterns can be extended without source edits — e.g., a comment marking the table as the canonical extension point, or a config-file/env-variable override — so new error signatures don't require modifying adapter source.
evidence: 'Normative pattern→enum routing table (first match wins; case-insensitive substring/regex over the transcript line)' — defined inline with no mention of configurability
'the classification enum ... and output schema are defined in the adapter module' — no external config file or extension point mentioned
findings.finding: The pattern→enum routing table and classification enum are specified as hardcoded in the adapter module, with no mention of configurability. Error-signature patterns for Claude Code transcripts are likely to evolve (new error types, new tooling), but the plan provides no mechanism to extend or override the routing table without modifying source code.
findings.location: Approach section — Normative pattern→enum routing table; `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py`
scenarios: A new error signature (e.g., a new LLM client error class) appears in transcripts and requires adding a new pattern — requires a source code edit and re-test rather than a config change
The enum itself needs a new value, requiring coordinated changes across adapter, tests, and any downstream consumers

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note (in AGENTS.md, a doc/ file, or inline module docstring) capturing: why the adapter is quarantined from the core package, what the confidence labeling scheme means, and how to add a parallel adapter — so the isolation contract survives contributor turnover.
evidence: 'To keep the harness portable, the adapter is optional and quarantined behind the backfill script — a parallel adapter could target another harness' — architectural decision with no doc update planned
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel isolated-backfill-adapter architectural pattern with a confidence-labeling scheme, but does not mention capturing this design decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc. Future contributors may not understand why the adapter is quarantined, what 'confidence=classified' means operationally, or how to add a parallel adapter for another harness.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a parallel adapter could target another harness'; Files section
scenarios: A contributor adds a second backfill adapter and unknowingly imports it from the core metrics package, violating the portability contract
The confidence-labeling scheme ('source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified') is misunderstood as authoritative rather than low-confidence, leading to incorrect metric interpretation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the backfill script: malformed JSONL lines should be skipped (with a logged warning), the script should fail gracefully if the transcript directory is absent, and writes should be idempotent (e.g., deduplicate by ts+kind before writing, or use an overwrite mode).
evidence: 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — new: targeted extraction (grep error signatures) -> classify -> write labeled snapshots' — no error-handling or idempotency mention
AC covers the happy path only ('asserts it emits the labeled classified records') — no error-path or duplicate-run AC
No mention of what happens on malformed JSONL, missing directory, or partial write
findings.finding: The backfill script performs file I/O (reading transcript JSONL, writing labeled snapshots) with no mention of error handling for malformed/partial JSONL lines, missing transcript directories, or write failures. There is also no mention of idempotency for the write operation — running the backfill script twice against the same input may produce duplicate records.
findings.location: `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`; AC — 'a test invokes its entry point over a fixture transcript dir and asserts it emits the labeled classified records'
scenarios: A transcript JSONL file has a corrupted line (truncated JSON) — the script crashes mid-run, leaving a partial output snapshot
The backfill script is run twice — output snapshots contain duplicate classified records, inflating metrics
The transcript directory is missing on a fresh clone — the script errors without a useful message
findings.suggested_fix: Add a try/except around each JSONL line parse (log + skip on JSONDecodeError), check for directory existence at startup, and either write to a temp file then atomic-rename or deduplicate on ts+kind before emitting to prevent duplicate records on re-runs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the portability-guard AC to also assert that `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` does not import `claude_transcripts`, covering the full set of modules that could inadvertently expose the adapter to the core package.
evidence: AC text: 'assert by scanning each module's source for the adapter import' — lists only registry.py and metrics/__init__.py
src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py exists and currently contains only a docstring and `from __future__ import annotations`, but is not listed in the scanned modules
ABSENCE: no AC assertion covers adapters/__init__.py
findings.finding: The portability-guard AC scans only `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` and `src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py` for the adapter import, but omits `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`. That file exists today, is part of the adapter package, and could inadvertently re-export `claude_transcripts` — which would make the module reachable via `import rebar.metrics.adapters` without tripping the stated guard.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — portability guard AC bullet
scenarios: A developer accidentally adds `from rebar.metrics.adapters import claude_transcripts` to adapters/__init__.py; the portability-guard test passes silently because it only scans registry.py and metrics/__init__.py.
The portability guard is meant to be the enforceable contract for isolation, but a gap in the scan set leaves one plausible re-export path unchecked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py` to the list of files the portability-guard test scans: 'assert by scanning each module's source for the adapter import' should cover registry.py, metrics/__init__.py, AND metrics/adapters/__init__.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `tooling` entry's `git` pattern in the normative routing table to use a word-boundary or more specific prefix (e.g., `\bgit\b` or patterns like `git commit`, `git push`, `fatal: not a git repository`) to prevent false positives on common words containing 'git' as a substring.
evidence: Plan text: '`tooling` ← `git` errors, `ruff`/`mypy`/`pytest` invocation failures, `Permission denied`'
Plan text: 'case-insensitive substring/regex over the transcript line'
The table is labeled 'Normative pattern→enum routing table (first match wins)' — implementers will treat these as authoritative specs
A transcript line like 'digital twin integration timeout' would match `git` (substring of 'digital') under the `tooling` rule before reaching `integration`
findings.finding: The normative routing table specifies 'git' as a bare case-insensitive substring pattern for the `tooling` category, but a plain substring match on 'git' will false-positive on common words containing that sequence (e.g., 'digital', 'agile', 'digits', 'agitated', log lines mentioning 'registry'). Because the table is explicitly labeled 'Normative,' implementers will use it verbatim, producing incorrect `tooling` classifications for unrelated errors.
findings.location: Approach section — Normative pattern→enum routing table, `tooling` row
scenarios: A log line reading 'Could not find digital certificate' matches 'git' (in 'digital') and is misclassified as `tooling` rather than unmatched.
Any log line mentioning 'registry' (contains 'git') would be misclassified as `tooling`.
Because `tooling` is checked before `none`, spurious matches inflate the `tooling` count rather than being dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare `git` entry with specific git-CLI error patterns: `fatal: not a git repository`, `fatal: `, `error: failed to push`, or use a word-boundary regex `\bgit\s` so only lines where `git` appears as a standalone word are matched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` processes the transcript JSONL in batches (e.g. N lines at a time) and flushes/commits incrementally, so large runs are interruptible and progress is observable.
evidence: Plan describes a single 'targeted pass: an error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' with no mention of batch size, chunk limits, or throttle.
ABSENCE: no mention of chunked reads, row-count limits, sleep/pause between writes, or progress checkpointing.
findings.finding: The backfill script has no batching or throttling: it processes the transcript JSONL in a single pass with no chunking, so a very large transcript file (months of sessions) produces one unbounded write burst to the NDJSON store with no progress reporting or pause points.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py (Approach section, 'targeted pass' description)
scenarios: A developer with many months of Claude Code sessions runs the backfill and the script OOMs or produces a single giant append burst to the NDJSON store, making it difficult to inspect or interrupt cleanly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--batch-size` option (defaulting to e.g. 500 lines) and flush writes after each batch; emit a progress line (e.g. `processed N/total lines`) so operators can monitor and interrupt safely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback/undo procedure to the plan (e.g. a `--dry-run` flag to preview output before writing, plus a documented compensating-forward step: `grep -v '"source": "backfill_classified"'` to produce a clean store), and assert the expected NDJSON store state after that procedure is applied.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan contains no mention of rollback, undo, cleanup, or compensating action if the backfill produces incorrect output.
The plan says the adapter is 'optional and quarantined' but does not describe how to expunge its records from the shared NDJSON store after the fact.
T11 rubric requires: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback' — the plan is silent on this.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no rollback or undo path for the backfill: if the script writes bad or unexpected records (e.g. due to a classifier bug), there is no described mechanism to remove them, and the plan does not assert the state of the NDJSON store after any rollback or undo operation.
findings.location: Approach section; Acceptance Criteria (no rollback AC)
scenarios: A classifier bug causes thousands of spurious `env_setup` records to be written. The developer wants to roll back but has no documented procedure — manual grep-and-rewrite of the NDJSON file is error-prone and undocumented.
DATA LOSS RISK: over-aggressive manual cleanup of the NDJSON file could accidentally remove authoritative (non-backfill) records interleaved with backfill records.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--dry-run` (emit to stdout, do not write) and `--undo` (rewrite the NDJSON file omitting all lines where `source == 'backfill_classified'`) flags to `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`. Document these in the plan's Approach section and add an AC asserting that `--undo` followed by a read of the store yields zero backfill records.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified fallback for malformed JSONL lines (e.g. skip-and-warn) and for entries with missing/invalid `ts` fields (e.g. emit `ts=null` with a warning, or skip the entry) so the adapter's behavior on imperfect real-world transcripts is deterministic.
evidence: Plan says the adapter performs 'error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' with no mention of error handling for unparseable lines
Emitted schema requires a `ts` field as 'ISO-8601 of the transcript entry' with no fallback if absent
ABSENCE: no 'skip and warn', 'emit ts=null', or 'abort' behavior is defined for malformed entries
findings.finding: No fallback behavior is specified for malformed or unparseable JSONL lines in the transcript input, nor for transcript entries missing or having an invalid `ts` field. These are real-world failure modes when the script runs against machine-local transcript files that may be partially written or corrupt.
findings.location: Approach section — Emitted record JSON schema; scripts/backfill_transcripts.py (described behavior)
scenarios: A partially-written transcript JSONL has a truncated final line; the adapter raises an unhandled JSONDecodeError.
A transcript entry lacks a timestamp field; the adapter emits a record with a null/missing ts, breaking ISO-8601 schema conformance.
The backfill script silently drops all records from a corrupt file with no user-visible warning.

findings.finding: AC2's portability guard claims 'the future _commands/metrics.py, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule' — but src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not exist (ticket 9a5a is open/undelivered), so the test asserting its source does not import adapters.claude_transcripts trivially passes by scanning a missing file (zero content = zero forbidden imports). The guard the plan asserts for this file is unenforceable within this plan's delivery scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (portability guard); 'the future _commands/metrics.py, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `_commands/metrics.py` clause from AC2 in this ticket. Add an explicit note: 'ticket 9a5a must add a portability-guard test asserting src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not import rebar.metrics.adapters.' This keeps the guard accurate to what actually exists at the time the test runs.

findings.finding: AC2 requires the portability-guard test to assert that `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` does not import `adapters.claude_transcripts`, but that file is created by ticket 9a5a which is NOT listed as a `depends_on` of this ticket. Until 9a5a lands, the file does not exist, so the source-scan assertion is vacuously true (or errors on a missing path). The guard is effectively a no-op for THIS ticket's test suite and provides no real enforcement until the dependent artifact lands.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 portability guard; Files section (src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py not in this ticket's file list)
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `_commands/metrics.py` portion of AC2 into ticket 9a5a's AC (which already has a portability-guard AC: 'a test asserts src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not import rebar.metrics.adapters'). This ticket's AC2 scope should cover only the files that exist at the time this ticket's tests run: `registry.py` and `metrics/__init__.py`.

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the output destination or rollback path for the labeled snapshot records emitted by scripts/backfill_transcripts.py. A partially-completed run leaves an incomplete output artifact with no stated back-out path, and the post-failure schema state is not described.
findings.location: Approach section ('write labeled snapshots'), AC1, AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Declare a fixed output path (e.g., `.rebar/backfill-transcripts.ndjson`), write atomically via a temp file + rename, and document in the plan that 'deleting the output file resets the backfill to a clean state.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
